event_blocks.summary: A deep-voiced male narrator introduces the 'Madrid's Galacticos era' as a controversial topic, questioning its success in transforming a club into a global brand versus its failure in building a balanced team, and whether business ambitions overshadowed footballing goals. The screen transitions into a nostalgic, cinematic montage of filmstrip-style frames, showcasing a man with glasses and a tie, a Real Madrid player in a white 'SIEMENS Mobile' jersey holding up a 'FIGO 10' jersey, and a group of Real Madrid players posing together. The montage also features a bald man in a grey top, interspersed with close-ups of smiling faces and dynamic action shots, all framed by a vintage filmstrip aesthetic. As the narrator concludes by stating this is the story of the Galacticos era's success and failure, a dramatic, suspenseful orchestral score begins to build. The sequence culminates in a stylized animation forming a circular 'balón' logo with a currency symbol, followed by a clear date display of 'May 20, 1998'. The segment ends abruptly with the distinct sound of a football being kicked, and a male voice clearly stating 'Roberto Carlos', followed by another voice saying 'no tire'.

event_blocks.summary: A vintage filmstrip animation overlays a grainy football match from May 20, 1998, accompanied by excited Spanish commentary celebrating Mijatovic's goal, crowd cheers, and a whistle, as a Real Madrid player in a white jersey lifts the Champions League trophy amidst jubilant teammates, with the commentary further noting Raúl's third goal securing an "impossible 3-0" victory over Valencia. The scene shifts to Real Madrid president Lorenzo Sanz, a man with glasses and a tie, first smiling while holding the trophy with a player, then confidently smoking a cigar against the backdrop of the Real Madrid crest. A narrator, over subtle, dramatic orchestral music, explains that after two Champions League titles in three years, President Lorenzo Sanz entered the 2000 presidential elections confidently, yet the filmstrip animation continues, revealing on-screen text of 'Total Debt €278M' as the narrator elaborates on "underlying problems" and "debt problems" totaling "278 million euros". The visual then transitions to a wide shot of the empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium, with 'REAL MADRID C.F.' displayed in the stands, as the narrator details the club's struggle to attract fans in the late 90s, noting the stadium was "almost never filled", with the final frame showing 'Average Attendance 99/00: 34241 Fans per match', reinforcing the narrator's point about declining fan engagement, all within the persistent filmstrip animation.

event_blocks.summary: A melancholic instrumental track underscores the opening aerial view of the dimly lit, empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium, displaying 'Average Attendance 99/00' with a rising fan count from 35,218 to 36,316. Florentino Pérez, a wealthy man who had lost the 1995 elections, saw an opportunity, with his portrait appearing alongside '1995 Elections' and a historical handshake photo. The narrator detailed Pérez's goal to save the club from its 'Total Debt €278M', symbolized by three Champions League trophies. Gazing contemplatively at a cityscape, Pérez made a historic promise: 'If I win the presidency, I’ll bring Luis Figo from Barcelona. And if I fail in that, I’ll pay the cost of all season tickets for the socios,' a promise that secured his win with over 55% of the votes, as evidenced by a newspaper clipping and his subsequent autograph signing for fans. Archival footage showed him being greeted by men in suits, including a bald man in a grey top, as Figo was presented days later, marking a new era for Real Madrid, culminating in the '00-01 Season'. Pérez aimed to replicate the glory of the 50s, inspired by ex-president Santiago Bernabéu who brought the best stars, like Di Stefano, to Madrid. Di Stefano was brought back as 'Honorary President,' and Luis Figo, symbolizing a pivotal transfer, agreed to sell 50% of his image rights. Pérez, on the phone beside a 'GANA en MADRID' sign, focused on clearing the club's debts, illustrated by a newspaper clipping stating '46.200 millones de deuda' and 'Guerra en el Madrid'. The sale of Fernando Redondo to Milan for 17 million euros was the first step, sparking protests like 'REDONDO NO SE VENDE' and 'F. PEREZ PESETERO' from fans and teammates. A quote from 'El Macao: Four Years with Real Madrid' explained Redondo’s public support for Sanz created animosity with Pérez, highlighting the club's political and emotional tensions.

event_blocks.summary: The 2000-01 season for Real Madrid began with a 1-0 victory over Valencia in January 2001, following a period where Luis Figo, having won the Ballon d'Or ahead of Zidane, was a key catalyst. Despite a 2-2 draw against Barcelona in March, which saw Real Madrid leading the league, a tactical diagram highlighted the attacking roles of Raúl and Guti, with manager Vicente del Bosque granting Guti attacking freedom, contributing to his best scoring season. The midfield duo of Makélélé and Helguera provided a rigid structure, though a moment of camaraderie was noted. After defeating Galatasaray in the Champions League quarter-finals, Real Madrid faced Bayern Munich, but a perceived arrogance and overconfidence, exemplified by minimal video analysis as noted by Steve McManaman, led to a home loss in the first leg due to Oliver Kahn's saves, and subsequent elimination from the competition. Financially, Madrid City Council approved Florentino Pérez's request to rezone the club's training ground from green land to a constructible area, significantly increasing its value and generating approximately €446.7 million, which cleared club debt and allowed for the construction of 'CIUDAD REAL MADRID' for €100 million. This financial maneuver enabled Pérez to invest further in the team, with the acquisition of Zinedine Zidane being his primary objective. After failed negotiations, Real Madrid broke the transfer record, signing Zidane from Juventus for €77.5 million, a move emphasized by the headline 'MISIÓN: ZIDANE' and a press conference announcing the historic transfer. The season culminated in a 5-0 La Liga victory over Alavés, leading to the team lifting the La Liga trophy, their first since 1997.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a Real Madrid player in a white 'Teka' jersey, mid-action, overlaid with a quote from 'El Macao: Four Years with Real Madrid.' A narrator explains this refers to Redondo's strong support for ex-president Lorenzo Sanz and his significant presence in the locker rooms. The scene transitions to a close-up of a bald man in a black shirt, identified by the narrator as Nicolas Anelka, with a graphic overlay indicating his €34.50m transfer to PSG after a single, troublesome season. This is followed by a newspaper clipping featuring Florentino Pérez with the headline 'Sobrevivir es un milagro' (Surviving is a miracle), as the narrator explains that previous transfers weren't enough to clear accumulated debt, and Pérez was reluctant to use the stock market option, indicating that financial operations would take time. The camera then pans across the grand, empty Santiago Bernabéu stadium. Next, a man in a suit and tie, identified on-screen and by the narrator as the new Sporting Director Jorge Valdano, appears in a close-up, followed by images of players Makélélé and Solari, whose transfers were made in the summer as part of many reinforcements. The video then cuts to Vicente del Bosque, labeled as Manager, with a serious expression, as the narrator states that under his leadership, Madrid started the season exceptionally well, with Figo being a main catalyst. The narrative shifts to grainy, blue-tinted archival footage of a Real Madrid match, showing a goal being scored and celebrated, followed by a crowd of fans in the stands. On-screen text identifies the match as '00/01 La Liga – GW6 BARCELONA – MADRID, OCT – 2000', while the narrator describes this as a tough, early test for Madrid at the fiery Camp Nou, following Figo's 'treason' as seen by the Culés. The final frames show a Real Madrid player in a white number 10 jersey celebrating with teammates, capturing the emotional high of a victory, as the narrator begins to describe the fans' welcome.

The video opens with a close-up of a Real Madrid player in a white number 10 jersey, captured mid-action and then celebrating a goal with teammates, accompanied by intense crowd noise and a commentator speaking of a "former star with passionate anger." A graphic then reveals the match result: '00/01 La Liga – GW6 BARCELONA 2-0 MADRID OCT – 2000'. The commentator explains that "Madrid lost the match by two goals and continued to lose two of the next three matches," sparking "endless criticism," visually reinforced by a stylized graphic featuring Real Madrid players R. Carlos and Casillas with the text 'El Madrid, a placer en peor'. However, the commentator quickly adds that "Los Merengues was back on track in no time, as they won an impressive eight consecutive matches in La Liga," as archival footage shows a goal being scored and celebrated by fans. A quote from Steve McManaman overlays the scene, where he states in his biography that the team prepared for European matches by watching only a single 15-minute video, suggesting a shift in mindset or strategy during this pivotal season for Real Madrid.

The segment opens with a close-up of Steve McManaman in a white Real Madrid jersey, as a narrator discusses the team's minimal video analysis, setting a tone of overconfidence. The video opens with a Google Earth satellite view of Madrid, highlighting the 'OLD TRAINING GROUND' with a black-and-white dashed outline and a cost label of '€446.7 MILLION'. A calm male narrator explains this deal generated "446.7 million euros," clearing club debt and enabling the construction of a new layout labeled 'CIUDAD REAL MADRID' for a "cheaper price" of '€100 MILLION', illustrating a major redevelopment project. event_blocks.summary: Florentino Pérez officially unveiled Zinedine Zidane with a white Real Madrid jersey bearing the number '5', as the club declared its ambition to be "the best in the world" and attract "global fans" to surpass Manchester United commercially. A young boy's excitement mirrored the club's aspirations, further emphasized by a graphic detailing Zidane's contract: 'Image Rights 20%' and 'Salary €5M/YR After Tax', with the narrator clarifying Zidane's agreement to exchange "90% of his image rights to Madrid" for "nearly 5 million euros a year after taxes." Pérez, seen signing autographs, and Madrid had planned a special year for their "100-year anniversary," as animated graphics of the Real Madrid crest from 1902 to 2002 played, accompanied by Carlos Pinedo's quote, 'Winning the club's first treble in our hundred-year history is our grand objective.' Archival footage of a 1960 match at Hampden Park, the site of the upcoming Champions League final where "Alfredo Di Stefano and co. won their fifth European Cup," was shown. Luis Figo's quote, 'We believe winning in Glasgow is sort of our destiny,' was displayed, with the narrator confirming Figo's belief that "La Novena will be their destiny." Vicente del Bosque, looking thoughtful, faced the strategic challenge: "But Del Bosque had a problem: Where will Zidane fit in the lineup?" The "Problema Zidane" was highlighted as the media questioned "where are the defensive transfers?" and noted Zidane's position at Juventus and for France was "non-existent at Madrid." Despite a poor start to the season, with Madrid "a single point away from the relegation spots after six matches" and a league table showing only 5 points from 15 games, "Madrid se tambalea" (Madrid is wobbling), Zidane's transfer was reinforced. Criticism targeted the Frenchman, especially after Madrid won their first four Champions League matches "without Zidane" due to suspension from being "sent off for Juve last season," as a Champions League form graphic showed Salgado with four consecutive wins. However, Zidane "quickly proved the critics wrong and finally showed his value," as excited Spanish commentators erupted with "Zidane!" and "Gol! Golazo de Zinedine Zidane!" celebrating his spectacular play. With uplifting background music, an English narrator stated, "Madrid were back on track after Del Bosque found the right lineup." A Real Madrid player in a white jersey scored, followed by the text 'Cambio de líder' (Change of leader) and a league table showing Real Madrid at the top with 35 points, having "snatched the lead in January after many important wins, and especially against Barcelona." The segment concluded with Figo scoring a late goal in a 2-0 win over Barcelona, marked by on-screen text '01/02 La Liga – GW11 MADRID 2-0 BARCELONA FIGO – 90+2'.

event_blocks.summary: A stylized graphic contrasting Real Madrid youth products with established stars sets the stage, as Florentino Pérez, smiling and signing autographs at a G-14 event, prioritized acquiring Ronaldo over defensive signings, particularly after his World Cup performance. A dramatic black-and-white newspaper headline, 'Peleazo Ronaldo,' over an image of Ronaldo in an Inter Milan jersey, precedes a clip of Ronaldo in a Brazil jersey celebrating a goal amidst crowd cheers and a commentator's excited exclamation, "And Ronaldo is rampant now!" Madrid's pursuit of Ronaldo began in August 2002, with Inter Milan president Massimo Moratti initially rejecting numerous offers. A newspaper clipping, 'Contacto inminente entre Florentino Pérez y Moratti,' signaled potential progress, but a split-screen graphic depicting Pérez and Moratti connected by a red 'X' over an arrow, alongside a gesturing man in a suit, illustrated a stalled negotiation, with Pérez needing more funds to meet Moratti's demands. The financial aspect intensified as '€15M' appeared next to Pérez, coinciding with Joan Gaspar's offer of 15 million euros for the out-of-favor Morientes. The concept of 'Opción Morientes' was then introduced with a black-and-white image of Morientes in a Real Madrid training kit, juxtaposed with a newspaper article, presenting him as a fallback option. The split-screen reappeared, now featuring Morientes's image, underscoring the transfer strategy and missed opportunities, while the narrator questioned Barcelona's involvement before concluding that Gaspar withdrew his offer an hour before the market's closure.

Man with glasses and tie.description: A man in a suit and tie, shown in the bottom-left panel of the split-screen, gesturing with his finger, likely representing a commentator or club official analyzing the transfer situation with a serious demeanor.

Massimo Moratti.description: A man with dark, wavy hair and glasses, shown smiling in a purple blazer, positioned opposite Florentino Pérez in a split-screen, representing the Inter Milan president who, according to the narrator, turned down many offers for Ronaldo and had specific demands for his transfer.

Opción Morientes.description: A black-and-white image of Morientes in a Real Madrid training kit, shown with a newspaper article in the background, highlighting him as a potential alternative signing for Real Madrid, with the narrator explaining that 15 million euros were offered for him as he was 'out of favor and didn't play much at all'.

Florentino Pérez.description: A man in a suit and tie with glasses, shown smiling while signing an autograph at a G-14 event, symbolizing his role as a powerful club president and his involvement in high-level football negotiations, with the narrator detailing his desire for Ronaldo and his attempts to secure additional funds to satisfy Moratti's demands for the transfer.

Split-screen negotiation graphic.description: A four-panel split-screen with Florentino Pérez and Massimo Moratti in the top panels, connected by a red 'X' over an arrow, and a man in a suit in the bottom-left, with financial figures and player images appearing dynamically, symbolizing the failed transfer negotiation and strategic alternatives.

Ronaldo.description: A bald man, initially seen in a black Inter Milan jersey under a 'Peleazo Ronaldo' headline, then in a yellow Brazil jersey celebrating a goal with a wide smile, accompanied by a commentator exclaiming 'And Ronaldo is rampant now!', and later walking away from the pitch, indicating his emotional and physical departure from Inter, with the narrator highlighting his return from injury and World Cup performance as reasons for Pérez's interest.

Real Madrid player in white jersey.description: A player in a white Real Madrid jersey, shown in the final frames with a black bar obscuring his eyes, overlaid with a quote from EL PAÍS criticizing the 'Zidanes y pavones' policy, symbolizing the human cost of the strategy.

Newspaper clipping.description: A black-and-white newspaper with the headline 'Contacto inminente entre Florentino Pérez y Moratti,' featuring a photo of Ronaldo walking away, emphasizing the media speculation around the transfer talks.

raw_segments: The video opens with a stylized graphic contrasting Real Madrid players Pavón and Portillo with legends like Casillas, Raúl, and Guti, as a narrator explains that not every youth product will be on par with these stars. Instead of buying defenders, Florentino Pérez, seen smiling and signing an autograph at a G-14 event, wanted Ronaldo to be the newest Galactico, especially after his return from injury and starring in the World Cup. A dramatic black-and-white newspaper headline 'Peleazo Ronaldo' over an image of Ronaldo in an Inter Milan jersey highlights a controversial moment, followed by a clip of Ronaldo in a yellow Brazil jersey celebrating a goal with a wide smile, accompanied by crowd cheering and a commentator exclaiming, "And Ronaldo is rampant now!" The narrator then details Madrid's interest in Ronaldo starting in August 2002, and after many offers were turned down by Inter Milan president Massimo Moratti, a newspaper clipping appears with the headline 'Contacto inminente entre Florentino Pérez y Moratti.' A split-screen then shows Pérez and Moratti, connected by a red 'X' over an arrow, symbolizing a failed deal, while a man in a suit gestures seriously, as the narrator explains Pérez wanted additional funds to satisfy Moratti's demands. The financial stakes are emphasized with '€15M' appearing next to Pérez’s image, as the narrator reveals that Joan Gaspar came in, offering 15 million euros for Morientes, who was out of favor. The final frames introduce 'Opción Morientes' with a black-and-white image of Morientes in a Real Madrid training kit, juxtaposed with a newspaper article, suggesting him as an alternative signing. The sequence ends with a return to the split-screen, now with Morientes’s image replacing the financial figure, reinforcing the narrative of transfer strategy and missed opportunities, with the narrator questioning why Barcelona was helping Madrid, before concluding that Gaspar withdrew his offer an hour before the market closed.

event_blocks.summary: A newspaper headline 'Morientes NO' signaled a failed transfer, with a narrator explaining a prior offer intended to prevent Florentino Pérez from funding a Ronaldo transfer. A split-screen negotiation graphic showed Florentino Pérez and Massimo Moratti, with a gesturing man, as the narrator detailed how Moratti buying Crespo saved Madrid €10 million due to a striker surplus. A financial arrow labeled '€36M' pointed from Moratti to a player image that changed to Ronaldo, indicating Ronaldo's price dropped, and Moratti agreed to sell for €35 million plus Solari, or €10 million in December. A central graphic depicted Ronaldo holding a 'RONALDO' sign between Pérez and Moratti, illustrating a €35M transfer deal, which was then crossed out with a red 'X' over '€10M' as Solari refused to transfer and Pérez had not paid the €10 million Moratti wanted. A newspaper headline 'TERREMOTO' with Ronaldo in a Real Madrid jersey followed by 'Llega lesionado' highlighted his injured arrival, confirmed by the narrator. Archival footage showed Ronaldo scoring two goals in a 5-2 victory over Alavés, with excited Spanish commentary shouting "GOL! de Ronaldo!" twice amidst crowd cheers, as the narrator described Ronaldo entering from the bench after missing three matches and scoring with expectations "through the roof." A newspaper headline 'Efecto Ronaldo' and a graphic of his goal statistics emphasized his immediate impact despite initial setbacks, though the narrator noted Madrid struggled in reality. A black-and-white newspaper graphic titled 'Ronaldo, negado como titular' displayed Ronaldo’s recent match statistics showing 0 goals in 435 minutes across five games, as a narrator explained his struggle to score in the upcoming months. A split-screen showed a newspaper headline 'Pitos en el Bernabéu al equipo blanco y a Ronaldo' on the left, and Ronaldo in a white Real Madrid jersey with 'RONALDO 11,' walking dejectedly on the right, with the narrator confirming he was whistled by the Bernabéu after a goalless draw. Footage cut to a November 2002 match between Barcelona and Madrid, where Ronaldo, in a white 'SIEMENS mobile' jersey, appeared frustrated walking off the field after a 0-0 draw, amidst loud crowd whistles and chants, with fans holding a banner referencing Luis Figo’s controversial move, as the narrator described Figo's hostile return to Camp Nou and the match ending in a stalemate. The scene shifted to a 2002 Intercontinental Cup match where Ronaldo scored a goal against Olimpia, celebrating with teammates, including Figo ('FIGO 10'), as the narrator detailed Madrid's trip to Tokyo and their title win. Ronaldo was then seen holding the 2002 Ballon d'Or trophy, with a graphic listing his 161 votes, as the narrator highlighted this award for his World Cup performances. The video showed Ronaldo in training and scoring a goal in a match against a blue and white team, followed by a close-up of his 'RONALDO 11' jersey as he celebrated, with the narrator noting his return to his best in the league, losing only a single game in 22 despite persistent fitness issues. A split-screen graphic announced the 'quarterfinals of the Champions League' between Manchester United and Real Madrid, featuring images of Sir Alex Ferguson and Vicente del Bosque, as a narrator discussed the match, which was 'filled with rumors about Beckham's potential summer transfer to Madrid.' A newspaper-style graphic with 'Galáctico' and a photo of David Beckham clapping set the tone for Real Madrid’s star-studded era. Archival footage showed a goal being scored in a packed stadium, followed by on-screen text indicating the 2002-03 Champions League quarterfinals, with Real Madrid defeating Manchester United 3-1 in April 2003. The narrator explained that 'after winning by three goals to one at the Santiago Bernabeu, the two teams entered Old Trafford to book a place in the next round.' Footage shifted to players entering the pitch, including a close-up of Ronaldo in a black Real Madrid jersey with 'RONALDO 11' on the back, as the narrator stated that 'all the questions surrounding Ronaldo from the fans were evaporated after a historic performance.' Ronaldo scored three goals in quick succession, each met with roaring crowd sounds and excited commentary, with shouts like 'Ronaldo shot!', 'Heartbreaker!', 'it's Ronaldo!', and 'What a hat-trick!' as on-screen text marked the score progression: 0-1, 1-2, and 2-3. Ronaldo celebrated each goal with open arms and a raised hand. A final graphic showed the aggregate score of 5-6 in favor of Real Madrid. A new graphic for the 'semifinals' of the Champions League pitted Juventus against Real Madrid, with images of Marcello Lippi and Vicente del Bosque, as the narrator noted that 'Madrid faced another tougher test in the semifinals against Juventus,' a series 'filled with absences and injuries.' A newspaper clipping headlined 'Raul accelerates his recovery to play in Turin' explained Raul missed the first leg of the Champions League semifinal, which Real Madrid won 2-1 at the Santiago Bernabeu. Live-action footage from the 2002-03 Champions League semifinals, 2nd leg in Turin, showed Real Madrid players in white jerseys, including Figo (number 10) and Ronaldo, celebrating a goal. The narrator detailed how 'Los Blancos' went to the Delle Alpi without Makelele due to suspension, conceded three goals, and Buffon saved Figo's penalty, a moment underscored by intense crowd cheering and a commentator shouting 'Buffon!' Juventus equalized with Del Piero scoring in the 43rd minute, updating the aggregate score to Juventus 3-2 Madrid. Despite Ronaldo entering the match after an injury, 'Los Merengues' lost, and the final whistle blew on a 3-3 draw in Turin, eliminating Real Madrid on away goals. Newspaper headlines like 'Máxima tensión' and 'Referéndum en el Bernabéu' reflected the intense public and media criticism that piled up on Del Bosque and captain Hierro, who was exposed athletically. The video cut to a montage of Real Madrid’s struggles, including a Copa del Rey quarterfinal loss to Mallorca (4-1), where they crashed out losing by four goals, and a La Liga defeat to Sociedad (4-2), described as a pivotal loss against the underdogs. Ronaldo was visibly dejected, and the segment concluded with a split-screen graphic contrasting Ronaldo and Nihat, symbolizing the team’s internal conflict and the emotional toll of the season’s failures.

The 03-04 season commences with dramatic music, and Florentino Pérez, alongside a smiling associate at a press event featuring Pepsi and Siemens logos, justifies the appointment of Carlos Queiroz, Alex Ferguson's assistant, as manager, citing a desire for a 'more tactically astute manager,' a sentiment echoed by a newspaper headline stating 'MÁS TECNIFICADO Y AVANZADO EN CUESTIONES TÁCTICAS.' event_blocks.summary: The 2004 Copa del Rey Final saw Real Madrid lose to Zaragoza 2-1 after extra time, sealed by Galletti's 112th-minute goal, met with crowd cheers. In March 2004, Real Madrid secured a 1-0 victory against Bayern Munich in the Champions League Round of 16, setting up a quarter-final clash with Monaco. A newspaper headline, '¡Y viene Morientes!', heralded the striker's arrival, followed by 'BALSAMO PARA LA CRISIS' over a celebrating Real Madrid player in a white jersey. The Champions League Quarter-Finals second leg against Monaco saw Real Madrid, already leading 4-2 from the first leg, increase their 'huge confidence' after Raúl scored in the first half, making Monaco require three goals. Intense play ensued, with Juli scoring a spectacular backheel goal ('¡Golazo! ¡Juli acaba de marcar el tercero de tacón!'), leading to an embrace among players in red and white jerseys amidst roaring crowds. Ultimately, Real Madrid 'fell on the hands of Juli and the loaned out Morientes.' The season concluded with a newspaper headline 'CRISIS' over a dejected player and a black-and-white image of a Real Madrid player being tackled, accompanied by the headline 'Broncazo del Bernabéu a un Madrid humillado por Osasuna,' reflecting a humiliating 0-3 La Liga defeat to Osasuna and fan backlash, as the narrator notes their subsequent loss. A dynamic background score underscored the team's emotional journey from triumph to crisis throughout the 2003-04 season.

event_blocks.summary: A stark newspaper headline, 'Broncazo del Bernabéu a un Madrid humillado por Osasuna,' underscored Real Madrid's humiliating 3-0 defeat to Osasuna, a loss contributing to a season-ending five-game losing streak that saw 'Los Blancos' plummet from second to fourth place in the league table between matchweeks 34 and 36. This decline was further emphasized by archival footage of a 2004 La Liga match (Madrid 1-2 Barcelona), capturing a goal and a dejected Raúl walking off, signifying the end of an era. Following Carlos Queiroz's sacking and Valdano's resignation, visually confirmed by the headline 'Valdano deja el Real Madrid,' Florentino Pérez, alongside a smiling Valdano at a press conference, appointed Antonio Camacho and acquired Samuel, a move noted by the headline 'Exhiben a Samuel sin haber firmado el contrato,' all orchestrated to generate positive press for the impending presidential elections. The '04-05 Season' commenced with a black screen and subtle, dramatic music, leading to Florentino Pérez's landslide re-election on July 11, 2004, evidenced by a graphic displaying his 94% vote share, as he pledged further team reinforcement, culminating in the Real Madrid crest against a grid background, symbolizing continuity and change.

raw_segments: The video opens with a stylized, grid-based graphic featuring the Real Madrid crest, as a narrator discusses transfer rumors, mentioning the team's interest in Emerson, Totti, and Vieira, especially after Conceição and Cambiasso were sold, thinning the defensive midfield. The scene transitions to a red-and-white pie chart labeled '50', which the narrator explains represents Real Madrid owning 50% of Samuel Eto'o's contract, as he was blossoming into a star at Mallorca. A close-up of Eto’o in a red training bib with 'Spanair' sponsorship is shown, followed by archival footage of him smiling and interacting with fans. A newspaper clipping appears with the headline 'Camacho no ve clara la incorporación de Eto’o', reinforcing the narrator's point that new manager Camacho couldn't see Eto'o as a starter, leading Madrid to sell him to Barcelona for 27 million euros after tense negotiations. The video then shifts to Eto’o in a green Cameroon jersey, smiling at a press event, before cutting to a training scene with David Beckham in a red and black England kit, wiping his face. The narrative continues with a graphic comparing transfer fees: Jonathan Woodgate at €18.3M and Michael Owen at €12.0M, both in Real Madrid kits, as the narrator confirms Madrid bought them, noting Woodgate was considered one of the best Premier League defenders. A man in a checkered suit, identified by the narrator as manager Camacho, gestures emphatically, reflecting his strict approach and desire for discipline on the field. He quickly identified Madrid's problems, but the solution could signal the end of his time at the capital. The final shot shows a match scene with the text '04/05 La Liga – GW3 ESPANYOL 1-0 MADRID SEP – 2004', capturing a moment of defeat for Real Madrid, which the narrator links to Camacho's potential departure after losing against Espanyol.

event_blocks.summary: The video depicts a period of significant turmoil for Real Madrid, opening with frustrated players on the bench, including Beckham and Raul, as the narrator notes this was the third league match they were benched. Newspaper clippings and text overlays reveal internal struggles, with quotes like 'Mi trabajo no se ve plasmado' and 'No le puedo sacar el máximo rendimiento al equipo' attributed to Antonio Camacho upon his resignation, indicating his work wasn't reflected and he couldn't maximize the team's potential. A stern-faced coach with a mustache, identified as Antonio Camacho, is shown, followed by serious-looking players David Beckham, Guti, and Zinedine Zidane. Florentino Perez's pressure to play stars is mentioned, alongside the challenges faced by new manager Ramon navigating unwritten rules. A split-screen graphic emphasizes strict discipline with the text 'punishment to whoever doesn’t comply,' featuring a man in a suit and a player in a white jersey. The team lineup in white 'SIEMENS mobile' jerseys is displayed, followed by a tactical diagram from November 20, 2004, detailing Madrid's formation against Barcelona at the Camp Nou, where Beckham and Guti were highlighted in red in a more defensive role, a strategy deemed ineffective. The stark match result 'BARCELONA 3-0 MADRID' is shown, with Ronaldinho and Eto's performances cited as key. A headline 'SACCHI, THE SHIELD OF FLORENTINO' appears, introducing Arrigo Sacchi, a bald man brought in by Perez as football director to resolve team issues. The narrative concludes with another poor result, 'MADRID 0-1 SEVILLA,' underscoring the team's struggles in the 2004-05 season, all underscored by dramatic background music.

raw_segments: The video opens with a tense scene of Real Madrid players on the bench, visibly frustrated and disengaged, with one player slumping forward in disappointment, as a narrator explains this was the third league match where Galacticos like Beckham and Raul were benched. The atmosphere shifts to a series of newspaper clippings and text overlays, highlighting internal team struggles with quotes like 'Mi trabajo no se ve plasmado' and 'No le puedo sacar el máximo rendimiento al equipo,' which the narrator attributes to Antonio Camacho upon his resignation, stating his work wasn't being reflected on the field and he couldn't get the most out of the team. A close-up of the stern-faced coach with a mustache, identified as Antonio Camacho, appears, followed by a trio of players—David Beckham, Guti, and Zinedine Zidane—looking serious and focused. The narrator then recounts Florentino Perez's hints about facing big problems if stars aren't played, and the challenges faced by newly appointed manager Ramon who tried to play by unwritten rules. This narrative transitions to a split-screen showing a man in a suit (likely a club official or commentator) and a player in a white jersey, with text emphasizing 'punishment to whoever doesn’t comply,' indicating strict discipline. The scene cuts to a full team lineup of Real Madrid players in white 'SIEMENS mobile' jerseys, standing in formation on the pitch, before shifting to a tactical diagram from November 20, 2004, showing player positions with Beckham and Guti highlighted in red, which the narrator details as Madrid's lineup against Barcelona at the Camp Nou, noting that Beckham and Guti in a more defensive role was never the best solution. The video then starkly displays the match result: 'BARCELONA 3-0 MADRID,' a significant defeat that the narrator attributes to Ronaldinho and Eto's performances. A newspaper headline 'SACCHI, THE SHIELD OF FLORENTINO' appears, followed by a close-up of a bald man, identified by the narrator as Arrigo Sacchi, whom Perez brought in as a football director to fix the team's issues. The final frames show another match result: 'MADRID 0-1 SEVILLA,' reinforcing the team’s poor form during the 2004-05 season, all set against a slightly dramatic background music.

event_blocks.summary: A newspaper headline 'ADIÓS CON BRONCA' signaled turmoil as Real Madrid lost at home to Sevilla, leading to Ramon's firing. Commentators discussed the situation, with one, identified as Sacchi, quoted as saying, 'If an aeroplane crashed in Colombia, he would blame the coach,' highlighting intense scrutiny. The Brazilian Luxemburgo, Real Madrid's third manager of the season, was introduced at a press conference with sponsor logos like Siemens and Audi visible. Previously Brazil's coach, Luxemburgo was shown holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'GRAVESEN 16', as Gravesen was brought in for defensive midfield balance. Early wins against Atlético, Zaragoza, and Mallorca were noted, but this was followed by a newspaper clipping 'ADIÓS MADRID', archival footage of a 1-1 Copa del Rey draw with Valladolid, and a 2-1 Champions League defeat to Juventus, marked by a dramatic goal celebration from Trezeguet in a Juventus jersey amid loud crowd cheers and excited commentary. The 2004-05 season saw Real Madrid in second place, trailing Barcelona in La Liga. A confrontation between Luis Figo and the manager, indicated by 'FIGO X LUX', signaled Figo's rift with Luxemburgo after being subbed in late in a match. Figo didn't start in the last seven matches, fueling departure rumors, and Real Madrid finished second behind champions Barcelona. The 05-06 season began with Figo still having a complicated relationship with Luxemburgo, as highlighted by a newspaper clipping 'Problemas para facturar a Figo' and his non-participation in preseason matches. Luxemburgo stated, 'If a footballer wants to leave, let him do so now,' indicating a new managerial stance.

Bald man in grey top.description: A bald man, initially seen in a split-screen and then in a close-up with a mischievous grin, pointing at his face, visually representing the quote about blame. He is later identified by the narrator as Luxemburgo, Real Madrid's new Brazilian manager, and is seen at a press conference and holding Gravesen's jersey.

Archival footage.description: Grainy, live-action footage of football matches, including a 2005 Copa del Rey game (Madrid 1-1 Valladolid) and a Champions League match (Juventus 2-1 Madrid), showing goals and player reactions, accompanied by crowd noise and a commentator's excited shout for Trezeguet's goal, providing context for the team’s struggles and early exits as described by the narrator.

Luxemburgo.description: The bald man, introduced by the narrator as Real Madrid's third manager, the Brazilian Luxemburgo, who had a great relationship with Brazilian players and previously coached the Brazilian national team. He is shown at a press conference with sponsor logos and later holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'GRAVESEN 16'.

A later league table shows Barcelona at 84 points and Real Madrid at 80, with the bold headline 'MISCA EL BARCA!', indicating Madrid finished second without a title, as stated by the narrator.

Coach in green vest.description: A man in a green 'SIEMENS' training vest holding a football, shown with a serious expression and the quote 'If a footballer wants to leave, let him do so now,' explicitly identified by the narrator as 'Luxemburgo,' representing his new managerial stance regarding player departures.

Luis Figo.description: A Real Madrid player in a white jersey with 'SIEMENS mobile' sponsorship, shown in a tense confrontation with another player, identified by the narrator as having a 'rift with the Brazilian manager' after being subbed in for only eight minutes. He is later seen distressed with hands over his face, and finally in a black shirt looking dejected, as the narrator explains he 'didn't start once in the last seven matches' and 'didn't participate in any match' in the new season, leading to 'many rumors on his departure from the club' and a 'complicated' relationship with the manager.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a dramatic newspaper headline 'ADIÓS CON BRONCA' (Goodbye with anger), setting a tone of turmoil as a narrator explains Real Madrid's immediate loss at home against Sevilla and the subsequent firing of Ramon. A split-screen shows a bald man and a man with glasses, likely commentators, discussing the situation. The bald man is then shown in a close-up, pointing at his face with a mischievous grin, as a quote appears: 'If an aeroplane crashed in Colombia, he would blame the coach.' The narrator clarifies this quote is attributed to Sacchi, highlighting the intense scrutiny and blame culture under the club’s leadership. The scene transitions to a press conference where the bald man, now identified by the narrator as the Brazilian Luxemburgo, Real Madrid's third manager of the season, stands beside others with sponsor logos like Siemens and Audi in the background, marking his arrival. A montage follows, showing Luxemburgo in a blue Brazil national team jacket, confirming his past role as Brazil's coach, before he is seen holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'GRAVESEN 16', as the narrator explains Gravesen was brought in to fill the defensive midfield spot and bring balance. A graphic lists early wins against teams like Atlético, Zaragoza, and Mallorca, supporting the narrator's mention of a winning streak. However, this quickly shifts to a newspaper clipping titled 'ADIÓS MADRID' and archival footage of a 2005 Copa del Rey match (Madrid 1-1 Valladolid) and a Champions League defeat to Juventus (2-1), as the narrator details their early exit from both competitions. The segment culminates with a dramatic goal celebration by Trezeguet in a Juventus jersey, accompanied by a loud crowd cheer and an excited commentator shouting "GOAL!", underscoring the team’s decline and the failure of Luxemburgo’s tenure, though the narrator briefly mentions a big 4-2 win in the Clasico at the very end.

The video opens with a dynamic montage of Real Madrid’s 2004-05 season struggles, beginning with a league table from April 10, 2005, displaying Real Madrid in second place with 63 points, trailing Barcelona’s 69, under the Spanish text 'Reválida a la presión blanca'. A narrator explains that despite Real Madrid's efforts "against Barcelona," the "Blaugrana still maintained a comfortable gap in the La Liga." This is followed by a tense face-to-face confrontation between two Real Madrid players in white jerseys, identified as Luis Figo, with the on-screen text 'FIGO X LUX' suggesting internal conflict. The narrator clarifies this moment "signaled the beginning of Figo's rift with the Brazilian manager, after being subbed in for the last eight minutes." A graphic then displays a series of match results against teams like Levante, Villarreal, and Real Racing Club, with times like '17’', '13’', and '0’', while a distressed Figo covers his face in frustration. The scene shifts to black-and-white footage of a Real Madrid player in a black 'SIEMENS mobile' jersey, as the narrator reveals "the Portuguese didn't start once in the last seven matches of the season, which opened the door to many rumors on his departure from the club." A subsequent league table shows Barcelona at 84 points and Real Madrid at 80, with the bold headline 'MISCA EL BARCA!', confirming the narrator's statement that "Madrid ended another season without a title, and in second place behind champions Barcelona." The segment transitions to a black screen with '05-06 Season' in white, accompanied by a shift in the background music to a more somber tone. The new season begins with a close-up of a dejected Figo in a black shirt, as the narrator explains "the new season started as the previous one has ended, as Figo's relationship was still complicated with the Brazilian manager." A newspaper clipping with the headline 'Problemas para facturar a Figo' highlights his contractual issues, further elaborated by the narrator stating that "after arriving with the team in preseason, Figo didn't participate in any match." The final frame features a coach in a green 'SIEMENS' training vest holding a football, accompanied by the quote 'If a footballer wants to leave, let him do so now,' which the narrator explicitly attributes to "Luxemburgo," solidifying the new managerial stance.

event_blocks.summary: A newspaper-style graphic titled 'FIGO ITALIANO' juxtaposed a smiling Luis Figo in a Real Madrid jersey against Samuel Eto’o holding an Inter Milan jersey, accompanied by Eto'o's quote, 'La experiencia ha sido negativa.' The narrator explained that Luis Figo, a 'Galactico,' left Real Madrid for Inter Milan, expressing disappointment with the club's leadership, and was joined by Samuel Eto’o and Solari at the Italian club. Subsequently, a headline 'El Newcastle, a por Owen' featured Michael Owen in a Newcastle United jersey, with the narrator noting Owen's return to England after a decent season. A split-screen then compared transfer fees, showing €20.0M for Baptista and €24.0M for Robinho, both in Real Madrid kits, as the narrator clarified that Real Madrid acquired multiple players like the Brazilian duo Baptista and Robinho rather than a single superstar. Sergio Ramos, holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'SERGIO RAMOS 4' and a transfer fee of €27.0M, was identified as the 'first Spanish Galactico' at 19 years old, with a release clause of 27 million euros. Match footage showed Real Madrid players in action, including a close-up of Jonathan Woodgate, who returned after a season-long injury, as the narrator commented on Madrid's average start. The narrative then detailed Woodgate scoring an 'own goal' in his 'first official match' and being sent off after two cautions. A final newspaper graphic titled 'DESPITE WOODGATE' displayed Real Madrid in second place in the league table, underscoring the team's resilience despite Woodgate's debut.

raw_segments: The video opens with a newspaper-style graphic titled 'FIGO ITALIANO,' juxtaposing a smiling Luis Figo in a Real Madrid jersey against a photo of Samuel Eto’o holding an Inter Milan jersey, with a quote from Samuel stating 'La experiencia ha sido negativa.' A narrator explains that this "Galactico left Los Blancos, as Luis Figo signed with Inter Milan," adding that Figo "was quoted saying that he was disappointed with the man who led the club," and that "both Samuel and Solari joined him at the Italian club as well." The scene transitions to a newspaper headline 'El Newcastle, a por Owen,' featuring Michael Owen in a Newcastle United jersey, with the narrator stating that "after a somewhat good season, Owen went back to England." The focus then shifts to a split-screen comparing transfer fees: €20.0M for Baptista and €24.0M for Robinho, both in Real Madrid kits. The narrator clarifies that "Los Blancos didn't bring a superstar, rather they brought numerous players like the Brazilian duo of Baptista and the young Robinho." The narrative continues with Sergio Ramos, shown holding a Real Madrid jersey with 'SERGIO RAMOS 4' and a transfer fee of €27.0M, smiling broadly. The narrator identifies him as "the player who was labeled the first Spanish Galactico, Sergio Ramos at only 19 years of age," noting his release clause was "worth 27 million euros." The narrative continues with match footage of Real Madrid players in action, including a close-up of a player with long hair (likely Woodgate) looking intense. The narrator comments that "Madrid's start to the season was average to say the least, as Jonathan Woodgate finally came back after a whole season out because of injuries." This is followed by a wide shot of a goal being scored, which the narrator reveals was an "own goal" by Woodgate in his "first official match," adding that he "was sent off after two cautions as well." The segment concludes with a newspaper graphic titled 'DESPITE WOODGATE,' showing a league table with Real Madrid in second place, and a photo of Woodgate in a yellow jersey, highlighting the team’s resilience despite his controversial debut, as the narrator begins to say, "But despite that, Los Blancos..." before the audio cuts off.

event_blocks.summary: The La Liga classification table shows Real Madrid leading with 15 points after match day 7, contrasting with Barcelona's poor start. During a November 2005 match, Ronaldinho, wearing his blue and red striped Barcelona jersey, scored in the 77th minute, securing a 3-0 victory as a Spanish commentator excitedly shouted 'Ronaldinho!', 'Gol!', and 'Golazo!'. The narrator observed that Ronaldinho 'demonstrated a lesson in football', and after celebrating by pointing skyward, he received a 'big ovation by the fans', which Salgado perceived as 'a way of punishing the Madrid players', while the Real Madrid goalkeeper appeared dejected. In the aftermath, a distressed Luxemburgo, the Real Madrid coach in a blue tracksuit, was 'sacked' after surviving only three more matches, as indicated by newspaper headlines announcing his departure ('LUXE, A LA CALLE') and Sacchi’s exit, with Juan Caro becoming caretaker manager and Sacchi leaving due to dissatisfaction. The segment concludes with a Winter Transfers graphic showing Cassano for €5.5M and Cicinho for €4.0M in Real Madrid’s white jerseys, symbolizing the club’s rebuilding efforts, though the narrator noted these transfers 'didn't change much at the club'.

League table.description: A dynamic graphic displaying Real Madrid’s position in La Liga with 15 points, 7 games played, 5 wins, and 0 draws, under the Spanish heading 'Clasificación', which the narrator confirms shows Real Madrid claiming the top spot after match day 7.

The narrator highlights how 'Ronaldinho demonstrated a lesson in football' and was given 'a big ovation by the fans', while a Spanish commentator excitedly shouts his name and 'Golazo!' during his scoring moment.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a La Liga classification table, highlighting Real Madrid at the top with 15 points after match day 7, as the narrator explains Barcelona's poor start to the season. The scene transitions to a match between Real Madrid and Barcelona in November 2005, where Ronaldinho, in his iconic blue and red striped Barcelona jersey, scores a goal in the 77th minute, leading to a 3-0 victory. During the goal, a Spanish commentator excitedly shouts 'Ronaldinho!' multiple times, followed by 'Gol!' and 'Golazo!', as the narrator remarks that Ronaldinho 'demonstrated a lesson in football'. Ronaldinho celebrates by pointing skyward, while the Real Madrid goalkeeper looks dejected. The narrator notes that 'The Brazilian was given a big ovation by the fans', though Salgado viewed it as 'a way of punishing the Madrid players'. The narrative then shifts to the aftermath, showing a close-up of a distressed Real Madrid coach, Luxemburgo, in a blue tracksuit, as the narrator explains he 'survived for three more matches' before being 'sacked'. This is followed by newspaper headlines announcing his departure ('LUXE, A LA CALLE') and Sacchi’s exit, with the narrator adding that Juan Caro became caretaker manager and Sacchi left due to dissatisfaction. The segment concludes with a graphic of winter transfers, featuring Cassano for €5.5M and Cicinho for €4.0M in Real Madrid’s white jerseys, symbolizing the club’s attempt to rebuild, though the narrator states these transfers 'didn't change much at the club'.

event_blocks.summary: In a historic Copa del Rey semi-final match on February 8, 2006, Real Madrid suffered a significant 6-1 defeat against Real Zaragoza, with Diego Milito scoring four goals, as evidenced by a dejected Iker Casillas and the prominent 'ZARAGOZA 6-1 MADRID' scoreline. The narrative then transitions to a Champions League round of 16 fixture against Arsenal, where Cristiano Ronaldo, after criticizing the Bernabéu crowd for not showing him love, witnessed Thierry Henry score, leading to a 'MADRID 0-1 ARSENAL' score. The segment concludes with criticism of Ronaldo's perceived selfishness, with Carlos Pinedo quoted as saying he 'thought more about himself than the team,' and Captain Raul reportedly criticizing his teammate for the same reason as Ronaldo and his teammates walked off the pitch in defeat.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a tense visual of Real Madrid’s Ronaldo looking downcast as a Zaragoza player gestures upward, setting a tone of conflict, while a male narrator, accompanied by a dramatic background score, details the "historic match" in early February where Madrid faced Real Zaragoza in the Copa del Rey semi-final. A graphic dated 'FEB 8, 2006' then appears, marking the date of this heavy defeat, as the narrator confirms Real Madrid "lost in a historic match by six goals to one." Visually, a dejected Casillas is shown alongside a composed Milito, with archival footage following of Casillas in a red jersey, visibly distressed, and a wide shot displaying the 'ZARAGOZA 6-1 MADRID' score. The narrator attributes "four goals by Diego Milito" to this loss, punctuated by a brief, excited Spanish commentary during a goal. The narrative shifts to a Champions League match against Arsenal, with players shaking hands pre-game, as the narrator explains Madrid faced a "tough test against Arsenal" in the round of 16. A close-up of Ronaldo’s intense gaze is accompanied by a quote overlay: 'Nunca he entendido a la afición del Bernabéu, que no es la misma que me demuestra cariño en la calle', with the narrator stating Ronaldo "criticized his own fans" and said the Bernabéu "never showed him any love." The crowd is shown waving white and purple flags, symbolizing fan support, before cutting to Henry scoring for Arsenal, with the score 'MADRID 0-1 ARSENAL' displayed, a goal the narrator credits to Henry. The segment concludes with a quote from Carlos Pinedo: 'Pensó más en él que en el equipo', criticizing Ronaldo’s self-centeredness, as footage of Ronaldo and teammates in white jerseys shows them walking off the pitch in defeat, with the narrator revealing that "Captain Raul criticized his Brazilian teammate" (Ronaldo) for "being selfish and thought more about himself than the team."

event_blocks.summary: Following a 2-1 defeat to Mallorca in the rain, dejected Real Madrid players in white 'SIEMENS' jerseys walked off the pitch, extending their gap from league leaders Barcelona to 10 points, as evidenced by a La Liga table showing Real Madrid in third place with 48 points. A newspaper graphic declared 'EL FINAL DE LA ERA GALÁCTICA' and 'DIMISIÓN' over Florentino Pérez, whose resignation was confirmed with his quote about spoiling the players. Although José Mourinho was shown speaking into a microphone with the quote 'From now on, I will play with players not names,' the narrator attributed this philosophy shift to manager Caro. A graphic of Mourinho shouting at various teams visually represented a series of six draws out of seven La Liga matches, which, coupled with their Champions League exit, ended their title hopes. Archival footage depicted a Real Madrid player scoring against Barcelona, followed by a dejected player after a 1-1 draw with Sociedad in April 2006, with the narrator also mentioning Ronaldo's 100th goal and subsequent injury. The segment concluded with Zinedine Zidane, bald and in a white Real Madrid jersey, at a press conference stating 'Au revoir' on April 25, 2006, confirming his announcement to retire after the summer World Cup.

event_blocks.summary: Zinedine Zidane is shown celebrating a goal in a black-and-white shot, marked 'ZIDANE – 66’', as a narrator declares the end of an era, noting the player's last match at the Santiago Bernabéu and foreshadowing a bleak future. A L'Équipe newspaper cover depicting Zidane waving further emphasizes his departure. The narrative shifts to black-and-white portraits, including a dejected Real Madrid player and Florentino Pérez, with the narrator explaining Pérez's success in transforming Real Madrid into a "commercial and profitable machine" despite a lack of titles and managerial turnover. A Deloitte graphic highlights Real Madrid's leading 'TOTAL REVENUES €M' for 2004/05 and 2005/06, confirming Madrid's top ranking in Deloitte's report on the world's richest clubs. A close-up of a smiling Florentino Pérez is accompanied by a pie chart detailing 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006', showing Broadcasting Revenues at 42%, Matchday at 30%, and Commercial at 28%. The narrator stresses that Pérez achieved this revenue shift, with broadcasting contracts forming the largest share, even after his resignation in February 2006, all underscored by dramatic, melancholic instrumental music.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white shot of Zinedine Zidane celebrating a goal, marked by on-screen text 'ZIDANE – 66’', as a narrator describes a player scoring in his last match at the Santiago Bernabéu, announcing "the end of an era" without good signs for the future. This transitions to a L'Équipe newspaper cover featuring Zidane waving, symbolizing his emotional farewell. The scene then shifts to black-and-white portraits, including a dejected Real Madrid player and Florentino Pérez in a suit, while the narrator explains that despite "titles having been absent" and "many managers and directors having been recycled," Florentino Pérez succeeded in his initial plan to turn Real Madrid into a "commercial and profitable machine." A Deloitte-branded graphic then displays Real Madrid leading 'TOTAL REVENUES €M' for 2004/05 and 2005/06, which the narrator confirms is based on "Deloitte's report on the richest clubs in the world," where Madrid claimed the top spot. A close-up of a smiling Florentino Pérez appears as a pie chart titled 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006' overlays the screen, detailing Broadcasting Revenues at 42%, Matchday at 30%, and Commercial at 28%. The narrator emphasizes that Pérez achieved this by "shifting and changing the revenue composition," with broadcasting contracts making up the "biggest share of revenue at the club" at 42%, even through his resignation in February 2006. A dramatic, slightly melancholic instrumental background music plays throughout the segment.

event_blocks.summary: A pie chart graphic from Deloitte, detailing 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006' with Commercial Revenues at 43%, Matchday Revenues at 26%, and Broadcasting Revenues at 31%, alongside a portrait of Florentino Pérez, illustrates the narrator's point that this revenue mix shifted in 2006, with commercial activities becoming the largest share at 43%. The 'Galácticos Policy' is then presented with a world map and images of Luis Figo, David Beckham, Zinedine Zidane, and Ronaldo, attributed by the narrator as a major factor in attracting global fans and driving this revenue shift. Following a colorful abstract transition, the narrator acknowledges differing opinions on the era's success. A black-and-white image shows Florentino Pérez smiling, contrasted with a serious portrait of him against blurred newspaper text bearing 'DIMISIÓN' and 'FLORENTINO PRESIDENTE', as the narrator explains Pérez resigned, losing players and fans, yet laid the financial blueprint for Real Madrid and maintained its health, eventually returning with a new perspective after acknowledging past mistakes. The scene abruptly shifts to live football action: a Real Madrid goalkeeper in a blue kit dives to make a save amidst intense Spanish commentary and a 'GOL!' sound effect, with 'UniCredit' visible on hoardings, followed by crowd cheering. This fades to black, leading into a credit screen with melancholic instrumental music, listing writer, director, narrator Fahad Al-Thekair, sources, and music credits.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a pie chart titled 'Madrid’s Revenue Mix 2005/2006', sourced from Deloitte Football Money League, displaying Commercial Revenues at 43%, Matchday Revenues at 26%, and Broadcasting Revenues at 31%, while a portrait of Florentino Pérez is visible. A male narrator explains that 'all of that shifted in 2006 as Madrid had its biggest share of revenues coming from commercial activities at 43%.' The scene transitions to a graphic titled 'The “Galácticos” Policy', featuring a world map and four Real Madrid players—Luis Figo, David Beckham, Zinedine Zidane, and Ronaldo—as the narrator attributes this shift 'mainly because of the Galácticos policy, which is a big factor in attracting global fans.' A colorful, abstract transition follows, during which the narrator reflects that 'when we determine the success and failure of this era, opinions may differ.' The video then shows a black-and-white shot of Florentino Pérez smiling beside another man, followed by a serious black-and-white portrait of Pérez against blurred newspaper text with 'DIMISIÓN' (Resignation) and 'FLORENTINO PRESIDENTE'. The narrator states that 'Pérez resigned and lost the players and the fans,' but 'he laid the blueprint for Los Blancos and maintained the financial health of the club for an extended period,' adding that 'he comes back in the following years with a new perspective, acknowledging his mistakes so that he succeeds again.' The scene abruptly cuts to live-action football footage of a goalkeeper in a blue kit diving to save a shot, with 'UniCredit' visible on advertising hoardings, accompanied by intense, fast-paced Spanish commentary and a 'GOL!' sound effect, followed by crowd cheering. This action fades to black, and a dramatic, slightly melancholic instrumental music track begins, playing over the final credit screen which lists the writer, director, narrator (Fahad Al-Thekair), sources, and music credits.
